title: Thermodynamics of bouncing grains
authors: Olivier Devauchelle, Predrag Popović, Piotr Szymczak, Anaïs Abramian, Arnaud Lazarus
abstract: When a horizontal plate vibrates strongly enough, it causes small particles such as sand grains to continually bounce on it and, over time, to diffuse across its surface. This phenomenon is the cause of the well-known Chladni figure, which is drawn by a higher density of grains gathering along the nodal lines of a resonating elastic plate. Using a heterogeneous, non-resonating plate, we investigate experimentally this type of diffusion. We find that, for the most part, is it comparable to classical molecular diffusion. We can define a temperature for the bouncing grains, and the system then obeys the fluctuation-dissipation theorem. We also recover Maxwell-Boltzmann statistics at equilibrium, when temperature is uniform. However, when temperature varies across the vibrating plate, the microscopic details of the grains' dynamics affect their macroscopic behavior: Fick's law, for instance, no longer applies. Instead, our experiments support a new transport relation that was recently proposed to represent diffusion in Chladni's experiment. Finally, we propose an expression for the heat flux associated to the non-equilibrium steady state predicted by this new relation, and test it against observations.
summary: This research uses bouncing sand grains on vibrating plates as a model system to study non-equilibrium diffusion. The authors demonstrate that when temperature gradients are present, the standard Fick's law fails, requiring an advanced stochastic calculus approach (the Itô process) for accurate description. They successfully measure and model the heat flux associated with this unique steady state.
main_problem: The study investigates diffusion dynamics of bouncing sand grains on a vibrating plate, specifically testing whether classical Fick's law or alternative stochastic models (like the Itô process) correctly describe non-equilibrium transport.
main_result: When temperature gradients are present, the system deviates from Fick's law; experiments support a new transport relation derived from an Itô random walk model. The heat flux associated with this non-equilibrium steady state is successfully measured and modeled.
method: The authors combine experimental tracking of individual grain trajectories on vibrating substrates with advanced stochastic calculus (Ito vs. anti-Ito integrals) to derive macroscopic transport equations.
model_or_system: The system consists of small particles bouncing on a vibrating, potentially heterogeneous substrate. The motion is modeled as a random walk process whose statistics are analyzed using concepts from non-equilibrium thermodynamics and stochastic calculus.
key_observables: Diffusivity (D), Root Mean Square velocity ($	ext{v}_{	ext{rms}}$), Heat Flux ($\dot{Q}$), and the relaxation parameter ($\gamma$).
important_parameters:
Vibration frequency ($
u$), tilt angle ($\phi$), substrate heterogeneity, and temperature gradient ($\Delta T/T$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the dynamics can be accurately described by specific stochastic integrals (favoring Itô over anti-Ito) and that the system reaches a non-equilibrium steady state.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate experimental setups, grain trajectories showing random walk behavior, velocity distributions fitting Maxwell-Boltzmann statistics, and plots comparing measured heat flux against theoretical predictions based on temperature gradients.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from basic observation of diffusion (comparable to molecular diffusion) to identifying the failure point of Fick's law under non-uniform conditions. It then rigorously compares stochastic calculus formulations (Ito vs. anti-Ito) and concludes by experimentally measuring and modeling the associated heat flux in the resulting non-equilibrium steady state.
why_interesting: The work provides a tangible, mesoscopic physical realization of complex statistical mechanics concepts—like distinguishing between different types of random walks (Itô vs. Fickian)—using simple mechanical systems, which has parallels to understanding energy dissipation and transport in open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. a: Experimental setup. The disk vibrates along the z-axis. The setup can be tilted by a few degrees around the x-axis. Quartz grains bounce randomly over a hard plastic surface (green, PVC), or a soft silicon polymer (pink, vinyl polysiloxane). b: Still frame from an experimental movie (run C). Dashed white lines show the grains’ trajectories during 1.5 s (75 frames). c: Idealized representation of the bouncing grains. Their trajectory during a jump is a free fall (dashed lines).
FIG. 2. The three substrates used in the experiment. a: Soft elastomer (pink, VPS). b: Hard plastic (green, PVC). c: Heterogeneous substrate.

FIG. 3. a: Examples of trajectories over hard and soft disk (runs L and M, f = 30 Hz, ds = 6 mm, dimensionless ac- celeration Aω2/g = 1.6 and 1.9 respectively). b: Average dispersion σ2 of trajectories with time (dots). Dashed lines show linear fits with slope 4D. Hard: D = 9.6 cm2 s−1; Soft: D = 20.2 cm2 s−1. Dotted gray line indicates box size R2. c: Autocorrelation function of the grains’ velocity (dots). Dashed line shows exponential fit, with amplitude from equation (5). Hard: D = 8.8 cm2 s−1, τc = 0.049 s; Soft: D = 19.9 cm2 s−1, τc = 0.073 s.

FIG. 4. Velocity distribution over a uniform substrate (runs L and M, f = 30 Hz, ds = 6 mm, dimensionless accelera- tion Aω2/g = 1.6 and 1.9 respectively). Colors correspond to type of substrate. Bars: experimental probability distri- bution. Dashed lines: Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution with the same root mean square value (vrms = 26.1 cm s−1 over the soft substrate, and 20.6 cm s−1 over the hard substrate).

FIG. 5. Empirical evaluation of the bouncing-grain model (section II, runs A to H only). All scales are logarithmic. a: Diffusivity as a function of root mean square velocity. Dashed line: equation (10) with a bouncing angle of θ = 75◦(fitted to data). b: Mobility as a function of root mean square velocity. The product mµ is independent from the mass m of a grain. Dashed line: equation (11) with θ = 75◦. c: Fluctuation- dissipation relation. Dashed line: one-to-one relation, equa- tion (12).
